speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I've-- Bent, I would like you to tell me what happened to your family when you returned to Denmark. Who and how many returned with you? And then go on with your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Like I say, there was four of my family that was-- we call it concentration camp, because that what it was. But my mom, Martha Lerno, my aunt, Olga Abrahamson, and my uncle, Leopold Abrahamson, and his brother, Elias Abrahamson all came back with the Danish Red Cross-- the White Buses, as they are now. There is a Bus in what was a Danish concentration camp in Denmark has now part of it being made into a museum. And one of the Buses are there. But I'll come back to all that, because that will come up later. Like I say, they took all the Danes that was there. Some snuck over and came with them. And you mentioned, too, there was a number. I thought there was 420. But that could be 430, however. Because I asked my mom things. And it was very hard to get things out of her. I can tell you she talked about when the Danish Red Cross, during the time of-- the war was going on-- came to inspect this place. Because they had made it like this was how all camps were in Germany. This was actually an old city which they had emptied. And now, they kept them all in, in those lousy places. There was like the slums of, of everything. And that's where they were. But, of course, it was better than if you had been in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother saw so many come to the camp and then leave again. And many, she could speak to. Because my mother spoke German fluently and a little Yiddish and, and some Hebrew. But this wasn't used much in Europe, you know? So my mother had seen atrocities, you know? They had being called out at four o'clock in the morning and stand because something was missing or something was happening. And she said there was a hanging going on and other things. But they never knew-- and when the Red Cross came to inspect it, at that day, everything was polished up. Everything was clean. They had a philharmonic orchestra that was playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had kids playing out in the park, a lake that they had cleaned up. There were, like, soccer games, you know. And there, there was candy standing out. The only thing were nobody could touch any of it till they were told to come over and serve. Then, then those Red Cross that came down was nothing but Danish Nazis. So of course, they wrote that, that they were well taken care of in these camps and how the Germans were. And that was used for propaganda. And at once, it was like if you go Universal Studio, you know, and you drive in those nice buses around. And you see all the fronts of all those nice buildings. The only thing it were, it was just the facades or all what was going on. So there that day, the Red Cross saw the front of Theresienstadt. And, and at once the buses left, everything was taken away. And nobody could touch anything. And it was back to being prisoners. And so, now you saw the backside of Universal. And that was how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life went on. And my mother had to work and cook and do kitchen duties and all. And a couple of times, my mother stole some of the, the food, you know? And even though it was pork, my mother said to, to all the kids and to whoever was sick, eat it. And you cannot-- the most important thing is to survive. So whatever we can get and eat and take, do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother-- again, I say her name, Martha Lerno-- was probably one of the key factors in-- that a lot of people knew her and knew. And she was, she was really with it. And even once she come back, I must say that my mother, of course, had suffered by it. But that was internally. And that-- you couldn't see through the outside. When time came, like I said, there was an hour before they were told that those buses are here for you guys. And, and that was it. But they didn't take anybody else and just whatever was Norwegian or Swedish and so on and so forth. And my uncle's brother was a bachelor, had met a, a lady. I can only say her first name. I know it was Hanke. She was from Czechoslovakia somewhere. And somehow, she got to Denmark with this group. And how she did it, I don't know. And my uncle's brother married her afterwards. And she became Hanke Abrahamson, so-- But that was how they were driven to Germany and into Denmark and then over to Sweden. And in Sweden, they went to health checks up and medical treatments and all for malnutrition and so on and so forth. Of course, they weren't as bad as [INAUDIBLE] at the border. And so there was French prisoners came up through Denmark. And if you saw bus fulls of just absolutely skeletons. Bones and skin was all-- and we were warned, do not give them anything, only if they want a cigarette. It was all we could give these-- they. they told us, do not give them food. Because they have to go through a whole regiment of, of food over the next year. And I have seen dead people, I mean, afterwards who are lying by the border and, and Germany. But this here was the worst of the worst that you really see, the piles of bones lying, the stiff bodies just lying around. This was just stiff bodies that were still alive. And I remember specifically giving a Frenchman a cigarette. And the only thing I got was just the eyes, a blink with the eyes. That was thank you, I presume. But so my mother had come to Sweden. And I didn't see my mother, of course, until-- and I was sent home from the military, which was toward the end of July of 1945. And we were sent home, and-- because we had give our uniform. So we had some old clothes on. And they had stored our clothes. So finally, I could change into regular street clothes. And then my mom and dad had been out walking. And I was standing outside our home in [INAUDIBLE]. And I saw my mother and my father come walking. And, of course, I must say that my mother and father have never really been-- because they had never learned to be excited, you know, in, in such a way, you know, that you show emotion or you show love. But I knew it was there. I went and hugged my mother, and, of course, shook hands with my father. Because that was what men did. You know, God forbid that you should kiss each other [? and all that. ?] And I taught them that later after I had been in the United States. But this was the first time that I met my mother since October of '43. That was in July of 1945. Our home was there. Our friends had kept it exactly as it was when we left. It just had to be cleaned up a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so life, in a sense, back to normalcy, but not that you could ever be back to normal again. Because I had lost friends. And I had lost part of my childhood, you know? And, of course, I would say some of the maybe most important years, from 16 till you are 18 when you really are grown up, and I did it in military duty, you know? And but I'm thankful for whatever was done for us. I would say, from your fellow Dane of different backgrounds, but we were all Danes. And I have maybe appreciated that more, I would say, after my now life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life going on. I became an apprentice again. And I got in, of course, to a different type of company. I got into a small company which was called I-B-U-L, Ibul, because my two bosses were young bosses. They had inherit the company from their father. And the one, his first name was Ib, I-B, and the other one was Ulf, U-L-F. So they used those two first four-- two letters each of the first name. And that became the company name. We made optical instruments, surveying instruments, binoculars and all. And that's where I finished my apprenticeship. And as my apprenticeship, I finished in 1947. I made a surveying instrument from the bottom up. The committee came-- you, you made the raw material halfway finished. And then they came and they stamped it with a deep stamp in them so that you couldn't cheat when you had to finish the instrument. I made my own lenses for the optical instrument. In those days we ground our own lenses. The cross-hair was used on lenses in those days were black widow spider cocoons. And you unrolled them until the spider, you know-- and that was put across a lens as a cross-hair. And I finished with a, with a nice mention on my certificate of, of master mechanic now, or instrument maker, as it was called. Of course, that became a good thing. Because in the United States, that became a, a very nice and noble trade. And so we had a funny incidence. I mean, we went to our summer town. And we enjoyed going back. And my two friends that lived there during the war time, the Mattsen, had now moved to another city in Denmark. And I will tell you how I met one of them later on, you know, and almost 50 years later. And in 1949, I had family in New York. My uncle and aunt, Max Itkin and Elsa Itkin and their son Charlie, had gone to the World's Fair in 1939. And they came on the shame ship as now Victor Borge, that time Borge Rosenbaum. Came to United States, to the World's Fair. And, of course, the war broke out. Now they couldn't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Borge Rosenbaum became Victor Borge, because he got a recording contract with the Victor Recording Company. They said, your name is too long, you know? And that was-- when they say his first name was good as his last name because it's [? Bro-er ?] in Danish. But because you don't have that line through the O, it becomes [? Bor-ger ?]. So they said Victor Borge. That was how his name came about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course my last name now, [? Layr-nu, ?] is also an O on the end with the two dots over or a line through it. So-- but when I came to United States, because I put on a line through the O, people erased it and thought that it shouldn't be there. So I learned just to put O, so that became [? Ler-no. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came-- my, my cousin and her husband, came to Denmark in 1945 to visit family then. At that time, he brought his big 1949 Cadillac with him to Copenhagen. And I was so impressed by this car and by, I should say, their coming to Denmark, that I thought it was just so-- my cousin looked at me. And I were out driving in his car. And I thought it was like I had gone to heaven. And I said, I'm going to buy one like that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my cousin and her husband said to me-- Joe and Gerry Collitt, C-O-L-L-I-T-T. They had a food [? delicate ?] store in New York that was called Old Denmark. It was on East 57-- 135 East 57th Street, right next to Lexington Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he said, why don't you come? You're single. You're young. Why don't you come to United States and see if you like it in the United States? So I said, OK. That sounds, sounds good. So I start filling out papers. Of course, in those days, it was nine months, you know, it took to fill out all the papers. And he had to send over guaranteed papers that when you come to the United State, he would be responsible for me and so I wasn't that the government had to pay me and so on and so forth. But I came to United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, the time had passed by. I came in 1951. On the 13th of January in 1951, I came to United States. I was supposed to have come in December, but the weather was so bad, they canceled the shipping that time. So I had to wait till the 7th-- 6 or 7 of January before we got out on And we sailed with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Normally, the, the, transportation that time took six days to sail across from Sweden to New York. But it took 13 days, because we head into storms. So I had left that time right after New Year. And we arrived deader than you can even think because. Even then, I were lying almost three days in a bed just hanging on. And then finally, the water smoothed out. And it was like glass, you know? And it was, it was very nice. So I came to New York. And I were picked up by a cousin, because we had been so late that not the whole family was there, just my cousin Charlie, Charlie Itkin. Worked, worked at that time for a big pen-- Everest, I think it was? were the company was called. And so he came down to East 57th Street pier and picked me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said the best place to, to stay is at the Sloan House. I didn't know what that was. There was a YMCA in-- on 34th Street in New York, and the worst place of many places. But it was cheap. So it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day I took a bus to-- up to my cousin's store. And he gave me a lunch. And I thought, gee, it would be nice to work here, you know? And he has a nice store here and everything else. And that would be great.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what, what happened was that after the lunch was-- I always remember was $1.61 with tax, he sent me over to-- we walked a quarter of a block over to Lexington. And he pointed down and said, that way down, four blocks down, is a-- Lexington Avenue is 54th Street. And you will see a sign outside that say, employment office. You go up there and just ask them for a job. I already called them and told them that you were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What am I going to do, you know? I went down there. This was on a Thursday, I think. And I went down there. And I went up to the employment agency. And in my Danish-German-English, which were pretty bad, I got myself a job as-- he thought I was an apprentice. I was trying to explain to him that I had finished my apprenticeship and so on. But he got me a job. And I were going to start the next Monday. So I went back. And we had found a Swedish guy-- oh, Danish guy-- [? Finn ?] [? Nilson ?] was his name. He was going to work for the United Nation. We got an apartment down-- or apartment room down in the Greenwich Village on Jane Street. So we moved down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I walked over to where I were going to work, because it was only four or five blocks over, then I were there, on 15th Street and 7th Avenue. And Jane Street was between 7 and 8 Avenue on 14th Street. So it wasn't that far to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I started there on Monday. And, of course, they thought I'm an apprentice, you know? So they gave me funny things to do like sweeping the floor, and cleaning up equipment, and et cetera, et cetera, until I finally started talking to the foreman. He was an old German that had been over 40 years in the United States in those days. So I spoke little German. And I brought in my certificate. And I said to him in German, I am not an apprentice. I am an full-fledged mechanic. And he looked at my certificate and said, oh, sehr gut. Then he took me into the office and he said, hey, we made a mistake, you know? He is a, a tool and die maker. He is full-fledged mechanic, you know? So in those days, they had started me on $37 a week. You know, I want from $37 a week to $137. And that was quite a difference, you know? So now I could begin to save a little bit of money. So I worked for a year and a half in the States. And I thought it was the greatest of all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but I knew that the paper they had given me only for a year and a half first. Then I had to go back to Denmark. So I went back. And right away, after a week, I went back into the American embassy. And I said to them at-- you know, I have such a great future in the United States. And I want to go back. But they said, the visa we gave you is that you have to stay nine months. But we will put in the papers for you so they will be ready in nine months. And so in the meantime, I got a job in Denmark. And then, at that time, my father wanted to go on a trip and my mother didn't. She didn't want to leave Denmark. So I went with my father in 1951, '52, I went with my father to Israel on a trip. I didn't realize that time that my father had already started getting sick. So when we finally arrived in Italy at the place after Venice, we went to Bari and Brindisi. And in Brindisi, we boarded a ship that was going to take us from Brindisi to Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first day out, when we sailed through the Corinth Canal and then into the Mediterranean Ocean, father said, I want to go home. What is all this water? Why we here? And I thought, what is wrong, you know, but of course, didn't know it. So when we came to Israel, I had to call my brother in Denmark. Because you couldn't buy a ticket in Israel. You had to buy it in Denmark and have it sent there. So he got a ticket to SAS Air just started flying there. And the next day, my father left Israel already. And I stayed there for a couple of weeks. I figured, I'm here, you know? And then when I came back, my family thought that I had taken all my father's money and everything else. And I said, you guys are crazy, you know? This is-- go and look in his overcoat. I stuck everything in his overcoat in the outside pocket. And my brother went down the hallway and opened the closet. There was his money, his papers, his passport, and everything else. That kind of upset me a little bit, because I had been away already now from the family for the second time. So I said, this is it. I'm leaving as soon as I can. Went to the embassy, begged and cried and said, I must leave, you know? And finally, I got the, the papers. So now I could go back to the same place I had been, you know? And-- but this time, I had the permit to stay. So I came back to New York. I stayed in New York, got a job, and worked there for awhile, and enjoyed the time. I'm a member of the Danish Soccer Club out to Bay Ridge, Long Island. And then I met some other Danes at the Sloan House in-- when we were down playing badminton. And they had just come back from Houston, Texas. And they said, oh, it's God's country, you know? And, and so I went to Houston, Texas. And we met this Dane that they had given the name and address. And he, the next day, sent us to a construction company that was owned by a Norwegian called [INAUDIBLE] Construction Company. And he hired us, boom, right on the spot, you know. And lied a, an inch or two, you know, but that was just-- they asked, can your drive trucks and can you do this? And we said yes to everything. And we figured, we are not going to say no.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, we had to meet way out of the city. And we didn't know we were going to build a bridge. And so the foreman was told by this Mr. [INAUDIBLE] that we were going to work there. And this was what we were going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy says, OK, see that truck down there? He said, I want you to go down and learn to drive that. And then drive it all the way out there. And see all those tree poles that is out there? He said, you go and pick them up and bring them up here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, there was two trucks that were doing that. So we saw how the other truck was hooking them on, you know. And we went down and with tape, we put on all the gear shift. And we were trying first what the gear shifts were doing. Because we, we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wrote a note on it and said, this one goes up. This one goes down. And all in Danish, you know? And stuck them on so we could read And when we had to have it go down, it came down, you know? And when it had to go up, we had to pull the other lever. And when we had to lift up the arm and then swing the, the big poles, you know? And then, of course, so that the poles wasn't swinging, we had a, a third helper, a black helper. Why I say now black was because I had never known that there was any difference until we came to Houston, Texas, that we had separate bathrooms and separate fountains you washed your hands in. So that was first time that we ever saw what discrimination was, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And oh, I could say that was the second time. First time when you found out that what it was to be a Jew and now what it was, too, that somebody had a different color than you, which we never considered. But anyway, we had a job. And I worked there two weeks. My friend worked there longer. He was a Swede. [? Gunnar ?] [? Shaline ?] was his name. He, he stayed with the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got a job in a big oil company instead. And that was indoors. And that was a much better job, you know? So I took that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in 1956, I left Texas. I had told them I were coming back. But I went back to my parents, because they had golden wedding anniversary. Fortunately or unfortunately, I met a girl there. And I married her. Her name was Marget Blackman. And I had to get paperwork first for her so I could bring her back to, to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then her family talked me into go to California, because my mother-in-law had a sister that lived in Los Angeles. So I figured, well, what am I going to do? In the meantime, in Copenhagen, when I came to my parents' wedding anniversary, I met an American. And I will tell you the name afterwards. But he said, if you ever come to California, here's my card. Call me up, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so for the time before I got married, I went out with this guy a lot of times in Copenhagen. We had a fantastic time. Now I went back to United States. And then I packed up all my things I had in Houston, Texas, and drove my old car to California and finally found the family near Olympic Boulevard on Wilshire Boulevard in Los Angeles. And her name-- and we came to the aunt-- was Lisa Leon,. And they lived on Plymouth Avenue in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed there with them for a couple of weeks till I could find my own apartment. But I figured I would wait till my wife, Marget, would come over. It took awhile because of getting her paperwork and so on. And finally, she came.
